explanation:
Applied physics solves real-world problems: from free fall to household electricity. Formulas are validated through centuries of experimentation.

## How It's Calculated

This calculator applies the free fall velocity formula with the values you input, checking against typical ranges and alerting you to any unreasonable values.

## Final Notes

This calculation is for reference purposes only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal, or engineering), consult with a qualified professional. Data is current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: Movement under gravity alone, with no other forces acting. On Earth, the acceleration is approximately 9.81 m/s².

faq.q: What is the free fall formula?
faq.a: v = g·t, where v is velocity, g is gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s² on Earth), and t is time in seconds.
This is Earth's average surface gravity. On the Moon, g ≈ 1.62 m/s². On Mars, g ≈ 3.71 m/s².

faq.q: Does the mass of an object affect free fall?
faq.a: No. In a vacuum, all objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass (Galileo's principle). A feather and hammer fall equally fast without air resistance.

faq.q: How fast do objects accelerate in free fall?
faq.a: They accelerate at 9.81 m/s² on Earth. This means velocity increases by 9.81 m/s for every second of free fall.

faq.q: How does air resistance affect free fall?
faq.a: This calculator ignores air resistance. In reality, air resistance eventually balances gravity, creating a maximum speed called terminal velocity (≈55 m/s for a falling human).

faq.q: Can you reach infinite speed in free fall?
faq.a: In a perfect vacuum, yes—there's no upper limit. But in Earth's atmosphere, air resistance limits most objects to terminal velocity.

faq.q: Are these results accurate enough for critical decisions?
faq.a: This is a reference tool using standard physics formulas. For critical applications (engineering, safety, research), consult a professional and account for real-world factors like air resistance.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology
